data.title: Ambarella raises fiscal 2026 revenue growth target to 31%-35% as edge AI demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ambarella (AMBA) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Feng-Ming Wang opened by highlighting, "Our strong momentum continued in our second quarter with revenue of $95.5 million, increasing 11% sequentially above the high end of our prior guidance range of $86 million to $94 million." He also announced, "With a strong order book as well as our expectation for both our total unit shipped and our average selling price to increase in fiscal 2026, we are increasing our fiscal 2026 revenue growth estimate to a range of 31% to 35% or approximately $379 million at the midpoint."
  • Wang reported, "We continue to see very strong demand for our new 5-nanometer AI SoCs in both our existing and emerging edge AI markets, which is driving our firm-wide average ASP price higher." He detailed three rapidly emerging applications: portable video, robotic aerial drones, and edge infrastructure, noting, "We expect to commence high-volume shipments into the robotics market by the end of this fiscal year."
  • Wang shared major customer wins, such as Arashi Vision launching the Antigravity A1 drone powered by Ambarella’s CV5 AI SoC and the company’s first win in edge AI infrastructure with the N1-655 SoC. Additional wins included Samsara’s AI Multicam platform, Audi and BAIC Stelato S9 for e-mirror functions, and Honeywell’s 50 Series security cameras in India.
  • Wang concluded, "Edge AI is expected to represent about 80% of our total revenue this year."
  • CFO John Young stated, "For fiscal Q2, revenue was $95.5 million, above the high end of our prior guidance range of $86 million to $94 million, up 11.2% from the prior quarter and up 49.9% year-over-year." He added, "Non-GAAP gross margin for fiscal Q2 was 60.5%, at the low end of our prior guidance range of 60.5% to 62% due to product mix."

Outlook

  • Wang announced, "The midpoint of our new third quarter and the full fiscal year 2026 revenue guidance range represents all-time record quarterly and fiscal year total revenue for Ambarella."
  • Young provided, "In Q3, we forecast revenue in the range of $100 million to $108 million or $104 million at the midpoint. Sequentially, we expect mid- to high single-digit percent growth in our automotive business with our IoT business up in the mid-teens. For fiscal 2026, we anticipate a revenue growth range of 31% to 35%."
  • Guidance for fiscal Q3 non-GAAP gross margin is expected to be in the range of 60% to 61.5% and non-GAAP OpEx in the third quarter is anticipated to be in the range of $54 million to $57 million.

Financial Results

  • The company reported, "Q2 net interest and other income was $2.2 million. Q2 non-GAAP tax provision was approximately $200,000. We reported a non-GAAP net profit of $6.4 million or $0.15 per diluted share in Q2."
  • Cash and marketable securities were $261.2 million at quarter end. Receivables days sales outstanding increased from 31 days in the prior quarter to 40 days, and days of inventory decreased from 98 days to 85 days. Operating cash inflow was $5.5 million, capital expenditures were $4.1 million, and free cash flow was $1.4 million.
  • WT Microelectronics represented 71% of revenue for the second quarter.

Q&A

  • Christopher Adam Jackson Rolland, Susquehanna: Questioned whether IoT could become a larger focus than automotive. Wang responded that the company will "continue to focus on those two areas," leveraging hardware architecture across both but acknowledged IoT is currently contributing more to income.
  • Kevin Edward Cassidy, Rosenblatt Securities: Asked about the drone market and process technology. Wang replied, "We will continue to work on the 2-nanometer project and still remain targeted to take our customers to production in early 2027."
  • Shadi Mitwalli, Needham & Company: Asked about Q3 guidance implying a seasonally down Q4. Wang explained, "Some of the products become driven by consumer cycles, that will definitely explain to you, that's why we are seeing the seasonality based on our guidance."
  • Liam Yevgeny Pharr, BofA Securities: Asked about M&A rumors and growth sustainability. Wang stated, "We just have no comment on that," and described continued growth for both IoT and automotive.
  • Kyle Robert Smith, Stifel: Inquired about inventory and customer demand. Wang said, "We haven't seen any inventory build beyond the normal practice."
  • David O'Connor, BNP Paribas: Asked about automotive ADAS adoption and growth drivers. Wang noted OEMs are focusing on "low end Level 2+ than higher end," while Young estimated growth is "roughly 50-50 between ASP and unit growth."
  • Auguste Philip Richard, Northland Capital Markets: Requested IoT split between security and nonsecurity. Wang said portable video is "definitely grow the fastest."
  • Zhihua Yang, Oppenheimer & Co.: Asked if portable video growth is driven by one customer. Wang credited multiple customers but identified Insta360 as the largest.
  • Richard Cutts Shannon, Craig-Hallum: Asked about the edge infrastructure pipeline. Wang confirmed engagement with "multiple customers, new and old or existing customer with potential design wins."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally positive, focusing on growth, market opportunities, and design wins, but included probing questions on seasonality, sustainability, and M&A rumors.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing momentum and strategic execution. During Q&A, Wang and Young provided detailed, occasionally cautious answers, particularly around future automotive growth and inventory checks. Confidence was shown with phrases like, "we are increasing our fiscal 2026 revenue growth estimate."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment among management was more optimistic and assertive, while analysts remained positive but increasingly focused on the sustainability and sources of growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q2 earnings revealed a higher revenue growth trajectory, with the fiscal 2026 revenue estimate raised from 19%-25% in Q1 to 31%-35% in Q2.
  • The emphasis on portable video, robotics, and edge infrastructure as new growth engines intensified, with more details provided on customer wins and applications.
  • IoT's outperformance relative to automotive was more pronounced, and management reiterated plans to increase resource allocation to IoT while maintaining a focus on automotive long-term.
  • Gross margin guidance was tightened downward slightly due to product mix compared to Q1.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautiously optimistic in Q1 to overtly confident in Q2, while analysts probed further into long-term sustainability and the potential for market shifts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted seasonality in Q4 due to consumer cycles.
  • No evidence of inventory build-ups was reported, with frequent checks in place.
  • The automotive sector faces slower OEM program decisions and longer time to revenue, with more rapid growth occurring in other edge AI applications.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the sustainability of growth, the impact of customer concentration, and the evolving market split between IoT and automotive.
  • M&A rumors were addressed with no comment from management.

Final Takeaway

Ambarella's Q2 2026 call signaled a significant acceleration in revenue growth expectations, driven by strong demand and expanding applications for edge AI across IoT, robotics, and infrastructure markets. The company raised its fiscal 2026 revenue growth estimate to a range of 31% to 35%, highlighted record edge AI revenue, and confirmed its leadership position in this technology segment. Management underscored ongoing innovation, a robust customer pipeline, and increased resource allocation for high-growth IoT opportunities, while maintaining a long-term commitment to the automotive market and rigorous inventory oversight.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
08:30 AM International Trade in Goods (Advance) 
 
This monthly report offers advance import and export data on the goods components of the monthly trade report. Goods make up roughly two-thirds of the nation's exports and roughly three-quarters of imports.
 
The consensus forecast looks for the merchandise trade gap at $87.7 billion in June versus $86.0 billion in June.
 
08:30 AM Personal Income and Outlays 
 
Personal income represents the income that households receive from all sources including wages and salaries, fringe benefits such as employer contributions to private pension plans, proprietors' income, income from rent, dividends and interest and transfer payments such as Social Security and unemployment compensation. 
 
Income expected up 0.4 percent and spending up 0.5 percent on the month. Core PCE prices seen up 0.3 percent versus 0.3 percent in June.  
 
08:30 AM Retail Inventories (Advance) 
 
Retail inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by retailers. The advance report is released late in the month for the following month and is part of the Monthly Advance Economic Indicators report. 
 
08:30 AM Wholesale Inventories (Advance) 
 
Wholesale inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by merchant wholesalers and are tracked to gauge inventory change in quarterly GDP.
 
09:45 AM Chicago PMI 
 
The Institute For Supply Management - Chicago compiles a survey and a composite diffusion index of business conditions in the Chicago area. 
 
Chicago PMI seen ebbing to 45.2 in August versus 47.1 in July.
 
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment 
 
The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.
 
The final University of Michigan index is expected unrevised at 58.6.
 
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. 
 
3:00 PM Farm Prices 
 
Farm prices are a leading indicator of food price changes in the producer and consumer price indices.

data.title: Ulta Beauty outlines $12B–$12.1B full-year sales target as new brand launches and international expansion drive momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ulta Beauty (ULTA) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Kecia L. Steelman, President and CEO, highlighted that "the Ulta Beauty team delivered significantly better than planned sales performance and continue to successfully execute our strategic priorities." She reported net sales increased 9.3% to $2.8 billion, operating profit at 12.4% of sales, and diluted EPS of $5.78. Steelman emphasized progress against the Ulta Beauty Unleashed strategy, noting, "comp sales growth of 6.7%, positive comp growth in both channels and all major categories, continued market share gains during a highly competitive quarter, loyalty member growth of 4% year-over-year to a record 45.8 million members and ongoing improvement across several key performance indicators." Fragrance led category performance with double-digit growth, and store and digital channels both contributed to comp growth.
  • Steelman announced the acquisition of Space NK in the U.K., entry into the U.K. and Mexico, and the upcoming store opening in the Middle East. She stated, "Our acquisition of the U.K. specialty beauty retailer, Space NK was a unique and strategically compelling opportunity to enter the growing U.K. market with an established and successful player."
  • Steelman also addressed the decision with Target: "earlier this month, we announced our mutual decision with Target not to extend our shop-in-shop partnership, which will conclude in August of 2026."
  • Interim CFO Chris Lialios said, "The Ulta Beauty team delivered strong performance this quarter, reflecting better-than-expected growth from comparable sales, favorable shrink results and merchandise margin expansion." He reported comparable sales increased 6.7%, driven by a 3.7% increase in transactions and a 2.9% increase in average ticket. He added, "For the quarter, consolidated gross margin increased 90 basis points to 39.2% of sales compared to 38.3% last year." Lialios also noted, "SG&A increased 15% to $742 million, including approximately $7 million of onetime transaction expenses related to the acquisition of Space NK."

Outlook

  • Ulta Beauty updated its 2025 outlook, with Steelman stating, "We have increased guidance for the year to reflect our strong first half performance and the impact of Space NK, which was not contemplated in our previous forecast." Lialios provided, "We now expect consolidated net sales for the year will be between $12 billion and $12.1 billion, with comp sales growth in the range of 2.5% to 3.5%. This outlook reflects our expectation that comp sales will be in the range of flat to up low single digits in the second half."
  • Lialios shared, "We now expect operating profit for the year will decrease in the high single-digit range and operating margin will be between 11.9% and 12% of sales." Diluted EPS is anticipated to be between $23.85 and $24.30 per share.
  • Lialios said, "We continue to expect gross margin for the year will deleverage primarily driven by store occupancy and supply chain costs, partially offset by lower shrink."

Financial Results

  • Lialios reported, "Consolidated net sales for the quarter increased 9.3% to $2.8 billion compared to $2.6 billion last year." Comparable sales increased 6.7%, transactions rose 3.7%, and average ticket grew 2.9%. E-commerce sales increased in the low double-digit range. Gross margin improved to 39.2%. Diluted earnings per share increased 9.1% to $5.78 per share. Inventory reached $2.4 billion, and capital expenditures were $77 million. Share repurchases totaled 245,000 shares for the quarter.
  • Operating profit was $345 million, and operating margin decreased to 12.4% of sales.

Q&A

  • Dana Lauren Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group, asked about the sustainability of the Ulta Beauty Unleashed plan and operating margins. Steelman responded, "We're going against some higher comps in the back half of the year... our guidance is reflecting the uncertainty that still is out there that remains with the consumer and the macroeconomic conditions." Lialios added that "many costs will likely deleverage inflationary pressures... primarily around healthcare cost, increased costs associated with our infrastructure investments."
  • Michael Charles Binetti, Evercore ISI, inquired about comp assumptions and margin leverage. Steelman replied, "while our comp performance through the first half of the year has been stronger, the operating environment continues to be really dynamic, and we think it's a little premature to change our long-term goals at this time."
  • Adrienne Eugenia Yih-Tennant, Barclays, questioned promotional activity and wellness. Steelman said, "the impact to gross margin from promotional offers was lower than second quarter of '24. We eliminated less productive events and overlapping offers... wellness can be a $1 billion business over time."
  • Additional analyst questions covered mass versus prestige category performance, Space NK acquisition rationale, recovery from competitive pressures, real estate strategy post-Target, and marketplace curation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was positive, with several congratulatory remarks but also showed caution, probing on guidance sustainability and the effects of macroeconomic pressures.
  • Management maintained a confident and controlled tone, frequently referencing prudent planning and strategic execution. Steelman expressed, "I'm very pleased with how the company is really rallying around our Ulta Beauty Unleashed plan."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was slightly more optimistic, reflecting stronger performance, but continued to emphasize caution for the second half. Analysts, while positive, asked more about sustainability and competitive pressures than previously.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 was raised from the previous quarter, with net sales outlook increasing from $11.5–$11.7 billion to $12–$12.1 billion and EPS guidance raised. Comp sales growth expectations for the year increased from flat to up 1.5% to a range of 2.5% to 3.5%.
  • Strategic focus shifted to include international expansion (Space NK acquisition and Mexico), the new marketplace initiative, and the upcoming end of the Target partnership.
  • Management's confidence was more pronounced, citing stronger comps and improved category performance, while still emphasizing prudence. Analysts focused more on long-term sustainability and margin implications compared to the previous quarter's emphasis on near-term uncertainty.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted macroeconomic uncertainty and ongoing consumer wallet pressures.
  • Lialios mentioned, "SG&A growth will be elevated in the second half reflecting both the shift of investment spending initially planned for the first half as well as the lapping of last year's expense trend."
  • Steelman identified that the ending of the Target partnership will require offsetting lost royalty revenue, but stated, "The partnership conclusion doesn't change how we're looking at our long-term financial targets."

Final Takeaway

Ulta Beauty management highlighted better-than-expected sales and comp growth, improved margin performance, and continued market share gains in Q2 2025. The company raised its full-year sales and EPS guidance, citing strong execution of its core business, successful new brand launches, and the strategic expansion into new international markets through the acquisition of Space NK and entry into Mexico and the Middle East. While expressing confidence in the Ulta Beauty Unleashed strategy and ongoing investments in marketing and digital, management remains cautious about the second half of the year due to macroeconomic uncertainty and rising costs, yet affirmed their commitment to delivering profitable long-term growth and returning value to shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Autodesk raises fiscal 2026 revenue guidance to $7.075B with 41% operating margin target as cloud and AI strategies accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Autodesk, Inc. (ADSK) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Anagnost highlighted strong second quarter results, stating "Revenue and non-GAAP earnings per share topped the higher end of our guidance ranges, billings, non-GAAP operating margin and free cash flow exceeded our expectations. As a result, we are raising our guidance for the full year." Anagnost emphasized the company's focus on cloud, platform, and AI strategies and reiterated that "we remain focused on executing our established strategic priorities in cloud, platform and AI, optimizing our sales and marketing to drive higher operating margins and allocating capital to organic investments, targeted and tuck-in acquisitions and continuing our share repurchase program as our free cash flow grows."
  • CFO Janesh Moorjani reported "Total revenue in the second quarter grew 17% as reported and 18% in constant currency." Moorjani explained, "Billings increased 36% as reported and 34% at constant currency, reflecting the shift to annual billings for most multiyear contracts and the transition to the new transaction model." He also noted, "Second quarter GAAP and non-GAAP operating margins were 25% and 39%, respectively, reflecting year-over-year increases of 240 and 140 basis points, respectively."
  • Anagnost discussed notable customer wins and technology momentum, including the sixth EBA with AtkinsRéalis and renewed agreements with Kimley-Horn and Dynamic Energy, all leveraging Autodesk's cloud platforms and AI-driven solutions.

Outlook

  • Moorjani stated, "We've raised our billings guidance range to $7.355 billion to $7.445 billion and raised our revenue guidance range to $7.025 billion to $7.075 billion, which flows through the strength of the business in the first half to our full year underlying guidance, along with some additional tailwinds from FX."
  • Non-GAAP operating margin guidance for the year is now "approximately 37% or approximately 40% on an underlying basis, which excludes the impact of the new transaction model."
  • Moorjani projected a "reported non-GAAP operating margin to be 41% in fiscal '29 or about 45% on an underlying basis."
  • Free cash flow guidance for fiscal '26 was raised to a range of $2.2 billion to $2.275 billion.
  • Share buyback targets for fiscal '26 were increased to between approximately $1.2 billion and $1.3 billion.

Financial Results

  • The contribution from the new transaction model to revenue was $105 million in Q2.
  • Billings benefited by $129 million from the new transaction model.
  • RPO of $7.3 billion and current RPO of $4.7 billion grew 24% and 20%, respectively.
  • Free cash flow in Q2 was $451 million.
  • 1.2 million shares were repurchased for $356 million at an average price of $298 per share.

Q&A

  • Saket Kalia, Barclays, asked about transformative M&A appetite. Anagnost responded, "we look at M&A as the next option. And we look at it for tech tuck-in reasons, really things that accelerate our existing road map and move us forward... These kinds of acquisitions tend to be in the hundreds of thousands to the billions of dollars range, not in the tens of billions of dollars range."
  • Adam Charles Borg, Stifel, asked about construction business momentum. Anagnost explained, "the momentum in our construction business is unchanged. We're seeing similar momentum to what we saw in the past. There's no deceleration. It's performing quite well... Our international performance is doing quite well, so we're growing well internationally."
  • Elizabeth Porter, Morgan Stanley, inquired about tariff risks. Anagnost replied, "we're not directly impacted by tariffs as a software company. But some of our customers still are struggling with the same things that you're hearing about pricing pressures and some of the things that are associated with higher cost of goods for some of their products. But they're all coping, and they're all coping quite well at this point."
  • Jason Celino, KeyBanc, questioned the drivers behind growth acceleration. Anagnost attributed this to AEC sector growth, strong EBAs, and performance in the Autodesk Store, highlighting the impact of the new transaction model.
  • Multiple analysts focused on the sustainability of new business growth, partner productivity, channel consolidation, and AI adoption, with management consistently stating performance is on track and improvements are expected to continue.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed on the sustainability of momentum, the effects of the new transaction model, and margin goals, with a generally positive to slightly positive tone, especially regarding operating leverage, AI, and cloud initiatives.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases such as "we are confident" and "we feel very good about the setup for the rest of the year." The tone remained consistent with prior quarters, with management emphasizing execution and discipline, and providing clear responses to analyst concerns.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone from both analysts and management was similarly steady, but with increased focus on long-term margin targets and AI strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Raised billings and revenue guidance for fiscal '26, up from the prior quarter's ranges.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin guidance was increased, and a new long-term target of 41% for fiscal '29 was introduced.
  • Free cash flow and share buyback targets for the year were both raised.
  • Management's confidence in operating leverage and sales and marketing optimization was reiterated, with greater detail on the expected progression of margin expansion.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on new transaction model impacts, macroeconomic uncertainty, and AI adoption, but this quarter saw more attention to channel productivity and direct business growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted persistent macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty but stated, "the macroeconomic environment seems broadly stable, but uncertainty remains elevated."
  • Challenges from the new transaction model are being managed, with Moorjani stating "operational friction from the new transaction model implementation continues to ease."
  • Analysts questioned the risk of disruption from tariffs, channel transitions, and renewal cycles, but management consistently reported that these risks are being mitigated and have not materially impacted results.

Final Takeaway

Autodesk management conveyed strong momentum in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, with revenue, margins, and cash flow outpacing guidance and prompting upward revisions across all key metrics. Strategic focus remains on cloud, platform, and AI initiatives, supported by disciplined capital allocation and ongoing optimization of sales and marketing. Management highlighted sustained customer demand, consistent channel productivity improvements, and a robust path toward higher operating margins and shareholder value creation in the years ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Marvell targets 36% revenue growth for Q3 2026 amid $2.5B auto divestiture and expanding AI market share
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Matthew J. Murphy reported record revenue of $2.006 billion for Q2 2026, reflecting a 6% sequential increase and 58% year-over-year growth, driven by strong AI demand in the data center end market. He highlighted, "Our data center end market continued its strong momentum, growing 69% year-over-year, fueled by robust AI demand. We also saw a solid recovery in our enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure end markets, which collectively grew 43% year-over-year."
  • Murphy announced the completed divestiture of the automotive Ethernet business in a $2.5 billion all-cash transaction, stating, "The auto divestiture aligns with our strategy to focus the company on what we expect to continue to be a massive AI opportunity in front of us by purposely redirecting our investments towards data center relative to our other end markets."
  • Two key management promotions were announced: Chris Koopmans to President and COO, and Sandeep Bharathi to President, Data Center Group, consolidating leadership to accelerate growth in AI and cloud markets.
  • Murphy detailed, "We hosted a highly successful custom silicon investor event in June, where we outlined an expanded $94 billion data center TAM for calendar 2028, a 26% increase from our prior view," and discussed winning additional sockets for custom AI silicon programs representing multibillion-dollar lifetime revenue potential.
  • CFO Willem A. Meintjes stated, "Revenue in the second quarter was $2.006 billion, growing 58% year-over-year and 6% sequentially. Data center was our largest end market, contributing 74% of total revenue."
  • Meintjes added, "Our non-GAAP operating margin was 34.8%. For the second quarter, GAAP earnings per diluted share was $0.22. Non-GAAP earnings per diluted share was $0.67, reflecting year-over-year growth of 123%."

Outlook

  • Murphy forecasted Q3 2026 total company revenue at $2.06 billion at the midpoint, representing 36% year-over-year growth, and noted, "Excluding revenue from automotive Ethernet, the implied revenue growth for Marvell's go-forward business would be closer to 40% year-over-year at the midpoint of our forecast for the third quarter."
  • Data center revenue is expected to be flat sequentially in Q3, with continued double-digit sequential growth in electro-optics, offset by lower custom revenue. Year-over-year data center revenue growth is projected in the mid-30% range.
  • Meintjes provided, "We expect our GAAP gross margin to be between 51.5% and 52%. We expect our non-GAAP gross margin to be between 59.5% and 60%. For the third quarter, we expect GAAP operating expenses to be approximately $719 million. We anticipate our non-GAAP operating expenses to be approximately $485 million."
  • Non-GAAP earnings per diluted share are expected in the range of $0.69 to $0.79 for Q3 2026.

Financial Results

  • The company delivered $462 million in operating cash flow for Q2, an increase from $333 million in Q1, and repurchased $200 million of stock in Q2, with $2 billion remaining in authorization.
  • Inventory at the end of Q2 was $1.05 billion, a decrease of $20 million from the prior quarter. Cash and cash equivalents stood at $1.2 billion. Total debt was $4.5 billion, with a gross debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.63x and a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.19x.

Q&A

  • Ross Clark Seymore, Deutsche Bank: Asked about lumpiness in custom business and Q3 headwinds. Murphy responded, "This is normal to see, particularly with the large hyperscale builds that happen and especially as you ramp them into production...you should expect a strong fourth quarter for custom."
  • Jeremy Lobyen Kwan, Stifel: Sought clarity on design win contributions and supply chain. Koopmans said, "We're seeing...more than I've ever seen in my 9 years at Marvell...some of the ones we're chasing now are much, much larger...just as these hyperscalers build out these rack scale infrastructure." On supply chain, Koopmans confirmed, "The supply chain is very tight...but we have very strong coordination and execution." Meintjes added no significant impact from tariffs has been seen.
  • Aaron Christopher Rakers, Wells Fargo: Inquired about customer concentration and timing for design wins. Murphy indicated, "There's a journey there from a handful to 18 plus to beyond...driving our market share from...10% share in '23, 13% the year after and driving to 20% over time."
  • Vivek Arya, BofA Securities: Asked about data center growth acceleration and alignment with industry growth rates. Murphy reiterated, "Custom would be up in the second half versus the first half...very strong recovery in the core business in enterprise networking and carrier."
  • James Edward Schneider, Goldman Sachs: Asked about capital allocation post-auto divestiture. Murphy said, "We are looking at how to deploy those...probably some of both" (buybacks and investments), and Meintjes added, "This additional capital really gives us a lot of flexibility around being opportunistic on doing more buybacks."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on lumpiness in custom business, design win timelines, and the impact of the auto divestiture, indicating a slightly positive but cautious tone, particularly around competitive dynamics and execution risk.
  • Management maintained a confident stance during both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing strategic wins and pipeline growth, as evidenced by Murphy stating, "We are very pleased with the progress of our 18 XPU and XPU attached sockets...the design win momentum in custom has been very, very strong even since the AI Day."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was more assertive on capital allocation following the auto divestiture and growth in custom silicon opportunities, with management projecting confidence in achieving long-term market share goals.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 revenue of $2.006 billion increased from $1.895 billion in Q1. Operating cash flow also rose, and non-GAAP EPS grew from $0.62 to $0.67.
  • The auto divestiture, completed ahead of schedule, marks a strategic shift to increase focus on data center and AI, with management reiterating plans to drive market share from 13% to 20% by calendar 2028.
  • Data center revenue proportion of total company revenue grew from 76% in Q1 to 74% in Q2, as the company prepared for new end market reporting structures.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on custom program visibility and competitive positioning, with Q2 showing greater emphasis on capital allocation and execution of the AI-centric strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Murphy pointed to lumpiness in the custom business as a normal consequence of hyperscale production ramps, indicating potential for quarter-to-quarter volatility.
  • Koopmans highlighted tight supply chain conditions, requiring strong execution.
  • Meintjes noted ongoing monitoring of tariffs, but no significant impact was seen to date.
  • Analysts raised concerns regarding timing and concentration of design wins, competitive claims in Asia, and the integration of new product lines.

Final Takeaway

Marvell emphasized its robust data center and AI growth, underpinned by record revenue, strong operating leverage, and a completed $2.5 billion auto divestiture. The company is intensifying its focus on the AI opportunity, projecting $2.06 billion in Q3 revenue and targeting long-term data center market share gains, supported by an expanding design win pipeline and disciplined capital allocation strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gap outlines $150M–$175M tariff headwind for 2025 while reaffirming 1%–2% net sales growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gap Inc. (GAP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard Dickson stated that "we are pleased to report second quarter results that over-delivered on our profit expectations and achieved our top line goals," noting six consecutive quarters of positive comp sales for the company and highlighting "significant gross margin expansion of 360 basis points to 41.2% in the second quarter versus the same period 2 years ago."
  • Dickson emphasized the strength of Old Navy, Gap, and Banana Republic, with Old Navy delivering a 2% comp, Gap posting a 4% comp, and Banana Republic achieving a 4% comp in Q2, while Athleta experienced a challenging quarter and is undergoing a "purposeful reset year."
  • Dickson announced the appointment of Maggie Gauger as the new President and CEO of Athleta, who previously held key leadership roles at Nike.
  • Dickson cited brand reinvigoration efforts, strategic pursuit of core categories such as denim and active, and collaborations as key drivers of momentum, particularly at Old Navy and Gap.
  • CFO Katrina O'Connell said, "our rigorous execution in the second quarter delivered solid results, surpassing our profit expectations and achieving our top line goals. The meaningful progress we're making across our strategic priorities continues to drive the business forward."
  • O'Connell added, "we are reiterating our fiscal 2025 outlook of net sales up 1% to 2% and are updating our expectations for an operating margin of 6.7% to 7%, which includes our estimated net tariff impact of approximately 100 to 110 basis points."

Outlook

  • O'Connell reaffirmed, "we continue to expect net sales to be up 1% to 2% year-over-year. Our outlook assumes ongoing strength at Old Navy, Gap and Banana Republic and a longer recovery at Athleta."
  • O'Connell provided guidance for gross margin, expecting it "to deleverage by approximately 70 to 90 basis points year-over-year driven by an estimated annual net tariff impact of approximately 100 to 110 basis points."
  • For operating margin, O'Connell projected "operating margin of approximately 6.7% to 7% for the full year which includes an estimated net impact of approximately $150 million to $175 million or approximately 100 to 110 basis points to operating margin."
  • The company expects third quarter net sales to be up 1.5% to 2.5% year-over-year.
  • O'Connell stated, "we do not currently expect the annualization of tariffs in 2026 to cause further operating income declines next year. And we expect to mitigate the full impact of tariffs over time."

Financial Results

  • Gap reported net sales of $3.7 billion, flat year-over-year, with comparable sales up 1%.
  • By brand, Old Navy net sales were $2.2 billion with a 2% comp, Gap $772 million with a 4% comp, Banana Republic $475 million with a 4% comp, and Athleta $300 million, down 11% with comparable sales down 9%.
  • Gross margin was 41.2%, down 140 basis points versus last year. O'Connell attributed this primarily to "the lapping of last year's credit card benefit" and incremental actions for Athleta's reset.
  • SG&A was $1.2 billion, or 33.4% of net sales, leveraging 130 basis points versus last year.
  • Operating margin was 7.8%, down 10 basis points compared to last year.
  • Earnings per share were $0.57, up 6% from last year's $0.54.
  • The company ended the quarter with $2.4 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, an increase of 13% from last year.
  • Gap returned $144 million to shareholders through repurchases and dividends and repurchased 3 million shares during the quarter for about $82 million.

Q&A

  • Alexandra Ann Straton, Morgan Stanley, asked about the rationale for lowering EBIT and EPS guidance despite Q2 outperformance and about tariff mitigation. O'Connell responded that the biggest update is including "$150 million to $175 million worth of tariff impact," but "without the tariffs, we would actually be expanding both gross margin and operating margin for the full year."
  • Marni Shapiro, The Retail Tracker, questioned if store improvements at Old Navy were more costly. Dickson replied, "we are not spending more in stores or in marketing. In fact, we're getting much more efficient and effective." Shapiro also asked about AUR at Gap, to which Dickson said, "the AUR is up, and it would be up without the collaborations."
  • Brooke Siler Roach, Goldman Sachs, inquired about confidence in Gap through the holiday season. Dickson cited campaign momentum and said, "Better in Denim is the #1 search on TikTok."
  • Matthew Robert Boss, JPMorgan, asked about revenue acceleration in Q3 and operating margin constraints for 2026. Dickson and O'Connell pointed to strong brand momentum and mitigation efforts for tariffs.
  • Lorraine Corrine Maikis Hutchinson, BofA, asked about pricing assumptions and why tariff pressures won’t ramp into next year. O'Connell explained, "targeted pricing is one of the levers that we're using to mitigate tariffs" and clarified proactive mitigation for 2026.
  • Dana Lauren Telsey, Telsey Group, sought comp drivers and marketing spend details. O'Connell said traffic was healthy and AUR was up, especially at Old Navy and Gap; Dickson noted, "we've been spending less and driving more effective results."
  • Irwin Bernard Boruchow, Wells Fargo, asked about gross margin components. O'Connell specified that "credit card is somewhere in that 80 to 90 basis point range... we also then missed our expectations based on... Athleta."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally neutral to slightly positive, with recurring questions focused on tariffs, margin outlook, and brand momentum, though some skepticism appeared regarding guidance decisions and tariff mitigation.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and brand momentum. In Q&A, O'Connell was measured and transparent, explaining tariff impacts and mitigation strategies, while Dickson was upbeat about brand progress.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a similar neutral-to-positive tone, but pressing more on tariff impact and guidance. Management’s confidence was consistent, though more defensive regarding tariff headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net sales growth remained at 1% to 2%, consistent with Q1.
  • Operating margin outlook was updated to 6.7% to 7% for the year, now explicitly including $150 million to $175 million in tariff impact, whereas prior guidance separated tariff effects.
  • Management’s strategic focus on brand reinvigoration and product categories like denim and active continued, with additional emphasis on cost discipline and efficiency gains.
  • Analysts shifted focus more heavily to tariff mitigation and margin sustainability versus previous questions on supply chain and channel performance.
  • Sentiment from both management and analysts remained confident but reflected heightened caution regarding external trade headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs and evolving trade policy represent a material headwind for the remainder of 2025, with an estimated net impact of $150 million to $175 million to operating income.
  • Athleta’s ongoing reset and underperformance in Q2, with a 9% comp decline and 11% drop in net sales, were highlighted as significant challenges.
  • Management cited efforts to mitigate tariff impacts through sourcing adjustments, pricing, and cost savings, but acknowledged the pressure on margins.
  • Inventory levels increased 9% year-over-year due to accelerated receipts and higher costs from tariffs.

Final Takeaway

Gap Inc. reported a solid second quarter, meeting top line goals and surpassing profit expectations while navigating a complex environment with new tariff headwinds. The company reaffirmed its 2025 net sales growth outlook of 1% to 2% and set an operating margin target of 6.7% to 7%, now inclusive of a $150 million to $175 million expected tariff impact. Strong brand performance at Old Navy, Gap, and Banana Republic offset continued weakness at Athleta, which is undergoing a strategic reset under new leadership. Management remains focused on sustaining momentum, driving efficiencies, and mitigating tariff risks, with clear confidence in the company’s brand reinvigoration strategy and long-term value creation potential.

data.title: SentinelOne outlines $998M–$1.02B FY26 revenue target as AI and platform adoption accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SentinelOne (S) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Tomer Weingarten indicated that "Q2 was a landmark quarter for SentinelOne. Our total ARR grew 24% and crossed $1 billion, a significant milestone in our growth journey." He highlighted record second quarter net new ARR, broad-based performance with strong customer additions, and increased adoption of AI and data solutions.
  • Weingarten announced the launch of SentinelOne Flex, describing it as "a unified flexible licensing model that gives organizations the agility to manage entitlements, seamlessly deploy new platform capabilities and drive greater return from their security investments." The Flex model has already delivered an 8-figure total deal value and is enabling larger, more efficient deals.
  • He also referenced the acquisition of Prompt Security, describing it as "a strategic step forward for us in one of the fastest emerging and most urgent domains in cybersecurity," targeting generative AI security and data leakage prevention.
  • CFO Barbara A. Larson stated, "Our total ARR grew 24% and surpassed $1 billion, a significant milestone in our growth journey. We added net new ARR of $53 million in the quarter, which significantly exceeded our expectations." She noted that revenue grew 22% year-over-year to $242 million, international markets represented 38% of total revenue, and operating margin improved by more than 500 basis points year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Larson provided guidance for Q3 revenue of approximately $256 million, reflecting 22% year-over-year growth. For fiscal year 2026, she stated, "we now expect revenue in the range of $998 million to $1.02 billion, $1 billion at the midpoint, representing 22% year-over-year growth."
  • She expects Q3 gross margin to be approximately 78.5% and for the full year, gross margin between 78.5% and 79%. Q3 operating margin is expected to be about 4% and full year operating margin about 3%.
  • Larson reiterated, "we are reaffirming our commitment to delivering positive free cash flow for the full year, which we expect to be a few points higher than operating margin."

Financial Results

  • SentinelOne reported total ARR surpassing $1 billion, net new ARR of $53 million, and revenue of $242 million for Q2.
  • International revenue grew 27% and constituted 38% of total revenue. Customers with ARR of $100,000 or more grew 23% to 1,513. The company maintained a gross margin of 79% and achieved an operating profitability of 2% in Q2. Net income margin increased to 5%.
  • Remaining performance obligations grew 26% to $1.2 billion. Free cash flow margin on a trailing 12-month basis was 2%, with $1.2 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and investments and no debt at quarter-end.
  • The purchase price for Prompt Security was approximately $180 million. Larson noted the acquisition's top line impact would be minimal in fiscal year 2026 with an estimated 80 basis point impact to full year operating margin.

Q&A

  • John Stephen DiFucci, Guggenheim: Asked about drivers of expansion and metrics to gauge platform adoption. Weingarten explained Q2 was "really broad-based with strong contributions both from new logos and expansion with existing customers," adding that "Purple AI and data, they remain our fastest-growing solutions."
  • Robbie David Owens, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on net new ARR trends for Q3. Larson responded, "We don't provide formal ARR guidance, but you can approximate it just based on our revenue outlook...Q2 results and the updated revenue outlook implies a relatively improved view on net new ARR for the full year."
  • Brad Alan Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Asked about Flex and its financial implications. Weingarten described customer flexibility and increased module consumption, while Larson stated Flex will "be very similar to our other ARR contracts, which is TCV divided by duration and revenue will be recognized ratably over the duration of the contract."
  • Joseph Anthony Gallo, Jefferies: Inquired about Prompt Security's revenue contribution. Weingarten described Prompt as addressing "a new class of risk" in generative AI and expressed expectation for significant contribution "in years to come."
  • Multiple analysts focused on drivers behind improved performance, Flex adoption, the impact of macro conditions, and margin outlook. Larson consistently mentioned maintaining prudent assumptions for the second half and taking a measured approach to guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a slightly positive to neutral tone, acknowledging improved momentum but pressing for specifics on ARR, revenue outlook, and the impact of new initiatives. Questions often focused on sustainability of growth and Flex's future impact.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, highlighting milestones and product adoption. During Q&A, their tone was measured and occasionally cautious, particularly when discussing guidance and macroeconomic factors. Phrases such as "we're being prudent" and "we're excited about the early momentum" were common.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed improved optimism, with less focus on macro-driven caution and more emphasis on product-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the introduction of the Flex licensing model and the Prompt Security acquisition, both strategic shifts not present last quarter.
  • Guidance language remains prudent, but revenue outlook for the second half was raised, contrasting with the previous quarter's more cautious stance.
  • Management's tone shifted from a defensive explanation of macro impacts last quarter to a more confident focus on execution and innovation this quarter. Analysts' tone moved from skepticism about ARR trends to interest in the scalability of new offerings.
  • Key metrics such as ARR, revenue, and margin all improved compared to Q1, with stronger expansion, record ARR, and operating profitability achieved.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted continued macroeconomic uncertainty, which "can influence deal timing and sales cycles."
  • Potential variability in the timing of larger deals was factored into guidance.
  • The top line impact from the Prompt Security acquisition is expected to be minimal in FY26, with an 80 basis point operating margin impact.
  • Analysts questioned the prudence of raised guidance given strong Q2 results, seeking reassurance around sustainability.

Final Takeaway

SentinelOne management emphasized that Q2 marked a significant step forward, with ARR surpassing $1 billion and strong adoption of both new and existing product suites. The company launched its Flex licensing model, closed an acquisition in the emerging GenAI security space, and raised its full-year revenue outlook to a midpoint of $1 billion. Management remains focused on delivering durable growth and operating leverage while acknowledging macroeconomic uncertainties and maintaining prudent guidance for the second half of the year.

data.title: Affirm signals ongoing acceleration in GMV and cardholder spend, eyes further growth through international expansion and AI-driven products
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Max Roth Levchin stated, "we actually set a new record in most of our metrics, which is unusual. Fiscal Q2 is a normal peak but this is Q4 and yes, it is the record. So that's really cool. I should tell you that our growth is accelerating and we are firing on all pistons." Levchin highlighted the team's tenure and affirmed his confidence in the company's trajectory.
  • Levchin underscored the ongoing expansion of Affirm Card, noting, "the current trailing 12 months of the cardholder is about $4,700. So I think the last time we dropped this number, it was along the lines of $3,500. This is across all Affirm services, so this is card and in all the other places where you might go, but card dominates that spend obviously. So we're not quite at the $7,500 but we're more than halfway there."
  • On new market initiatives, Levchin shared that Affirm is "in friends-and-family testing in the U.K. with our Shopify friends. It's very exciting so that's obviously an enormous potential of that is not lost on anyone."
  • COO Michael Linford commented on capital partnerships, "we partner with who we think is the blue chips of these asset managers. And that can come in the form of large strategic partnerships with world-class investors like Sixth Street or very good insurance asset managers up and down our stack."
  • CFO Robert W. O'Hare added, "given the short duration of the loans that we're originating, the most important thing for us is that we have a full picture of the borrower's wherewithal to repay the loan at the time of origination. And then the asset is so short-dated and we're increasingly working with consumers that we've seen before. 95% of our transactions came from repeat borrowers this quarter."

Outlook

  • Affirm leadership maintained a positive outlook on both growth and profitability, with Levchin stating, "from the consumer point of view... we think that it continues to perform. It's really maybe a commentary on how strong the momentum is in the U.S., and to at least a similar degree, Canadian consumer and soon, we'll find out what that looks like for U.K. one."
  • The company anticipates continued acceleration in GMV and further expansion of the 0% APR program, with Levchin noting, "monthly 0% loans were growing north of 90% year-on-year. So we would expect that, that loan product in particular, continues to take a bit of share within our mix."
  • The international rollout, particularly in the U.K. with Shopify, is positioned as a near-term growth lever. Levchin indicated, "as we scale Shopify, that is absolutely subject to change just based on what this will do relative to what's available."

Financial Results

  • Levchin described Q4 as a record-setting quarter for most metrics.
  • O'Hare reported that "95% of our transactions came from repeat borrowers this quarter," highlighting strong customer engagement and low risk for new originations.
  • Affirm Card volume reached $1.2 billion with a 10% attach rate, and the trailing 12-month cardholder spend increased to $4,700 across Affirm services.
  • Monthly 0% APR loans grew more than 90% year-on-year, and the number of merchants funding the offering doubled to 7% of the merchant base.

Q&A

  • Dan Dolev, Mizuho: Asked about improved optimism versus last quarter and drivers for the uptick. Levchin responded that demand for service and credit performance remain strong, and originations are accelerating.
  • Daniel Rock Perlin, RBC: Queried repeat rates for first-time 0% APR users. Levchin replied, "there's not a tremendous difference in terms of repeat of users that have been acquired through 0s or not. But... do 0% users flip over to interest bearing? And they do."
  • Adam Frisch, Evercore ISI: Questioned consumer risk and FICO distribution amid potential headwinds. Levchin explained the company is "not concerned" due to real-time underwriting and ongoing credit monitoring, emphasizing that "credit is job #1."
  • Will Nance, Goldman Sachs: Asked about risk from alternative lenders with increased funding. Linford stressed Affirm's strategy of choosing "blue chips of these asset managers" and maintaining long-term discipline.
  • Multiple analysts, including Adib Choudhury (William Blair), pressed on international and U.K. growth. Levchin confirmed the U.K. launch is underway, with early testing and plans to leverage Shopify for rapid scaling.
  • Rob Wildhack, Autonomous Research: Inquired about the 0% APR strategy. Levchin attributed Affirm's success to underwriting strength and the ability to structure merchant-subsidized offers profitably.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive, frequently using phrases like "crushing it" and "great results," but their questions often probed sustainability of current trends, competitive risks, and consumer health.
  • Management displayed high confidence, using phrases such as "we are firing on all pistons," and providing detailed, assertive responses. Levchin often emphasized control over credit risk and growth levers.
  • The tone remained highly confident in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with management maintaining more optimism this quarter compared to last, when there was more caution around macro risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 featured more assertive claims of record results and accelerating growth, compared to Q3's more cautious optimism and focus on judicious growth.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize rapid expansion, especially in 0% APR products and international markets.
  • The focus of analyst questions evolved from managing risk and competition (previous quarter) to probing Affirm's ability to sustain growth and capitalize on new markets and partnerships.
  • Management's confidence in credit quality and underwriting remains strong, but this quarter, the emphasis shifted to growth acceleration and the scaling of the Affirm Card.
  • The share of repeat borrowers and engagement with new products (especially the card) were more frequently highlighted.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing monitoring of macroeconomic shifts, including student loan repayments and consumer credit health.
  • Affirm continues to prioritize credit risk management, with Levchin reaffirming, "credit is job #1." The team is prepared to adjust underwriting standards as needed.
  • Analyst concerns centered on competitive pressures from alternative lenders, risk in funding markets, and the sustainability of high growth in 0% APR and card products.
  • The upcoming transition of a major enterprise merchant was addressed, with the outlook assuming "the integration goes away at the end of this quarter" and volume from that partner to be conservatively modeled.

Final Takeaway

Affirm Holdings delivered a record-setting quarter, highlighted by accelerating growth in GMV, substantial increases in Affirm Card usage, and the rapid expansion of its 0% APR product. Management emphasized the strength of its underwriting, the durability of consumer demand, and the company’s readiness to capitalize on international opportunities, particularly in the U.K. with Shopify. Repeat usage remains high, and new product initiatives, including AI-driven checkout optimization, signal a focus on both innovation and disciplined risk management as Affirm moves into its next phase of growth.

data.title: Caterpillar raises expected impact of tariffs, says full-year costs could hit $1.8B
data.description:
Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) -2.7% post-market Thursday after disclosing it now expects the net impact from tariffs in the $1.5B-$1.8B range this year, up from a previous estimate of $1.3B-$1.5B, as well as a $500M-$600M impact in Q3, compared to its earlier outlook for $400M-$500M.

Including the net impact from incremental tariffs, Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) said in an 8-K filing that it now expects its full-year adjusted operating profit margin to come in near the bottom of the target margin range, although it left its full-year sales outlook unchanged.

"While the company continues to take initial mitigating actions to reduce this impact, trade and tariff negotiations continue to be fluid," Caterpillar (CAT) said.

data.title: India poised to increase Russian energy imports, defying U.S. demands
data.description:
The Trump administration's steep tariffs on India have not forced the country to stop buying Russian oil, as Reuters reported Thursday that Indian imports of Russian oil are expected to rise next month.

Indian refiners plan to increase their purchases of Russian oil in September by 10%-20% compared to August levels, or by an additional 150K-300K bbl/day, according to Reuters, which cited trading sources involved in the Indian market.

India reportedly imported 1.5M bbl/day of oil from Russia during the first 20 days of August, unchanged from July and slightly below the average of 1.6M bbl/day in the January-June period; the volumes equal 1.5% of global supply, making India the largest buyer of seaborne Russian crude, which covers ~40% of India's oil needs.

India likely will remain a major buyer of Russian oil despite 50% U.S. tariffs on Indian imports that started Wednesday, Commonwealth Bank of Australia analyst Vivek Dhar said in a note.

Although ship tracking data indicates Russian exports to India have eased by 15%‑20% since late June, the lack of an official ban or reduction of purchases reflects "the political tightrope India is trying to walk," Dhar wrote. "India is trying to make some concessions to U.S. demands, while also trying to keep its relationship with Russia intact."

India's oil refineries likely will largely continue to purchase Russian oil to maximize profits as it trades at a slight discount to Brent oil, Dhar said.

Crude oil futures settled higher Thursday, with the market watching increased drone strikes in the Russia-Ukraine war just weeks after hopes were raised for peace talks.

German Chancellor Merz told reporters that a meeting between Ukraine's President Zelenskiy and Russia's President Putin "won't happen."

Meanwhile, Citi maintained its average Brent price forecast at $66/bbl for Q3 and$63/bbl in Q4 as "oversupply should start to exert greater impact on the oil supply-demand balance into year end."

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for October delivery finished +0.7% to $64.60/bbl, front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for October delivery closed +0.8% to $68.62/bbl, and front-month Nymex natural gas for October - the new front month - ended +2% to $2.944/MMBtu, its third straight daily gain.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (NYSEARCA:XLE)
